statement: All 32 source PDFs in the BenchCore document set must have corresponding inventory entries in the benchmark inventory file. Each entry must include the PDF filename, source URL or origin path, content hash (SHA-256), page count, and a brief description of the document's relevance to the benchmark. Missing or incomplete inventory entries constitute a data quality failure.

All requirement-bearing visuals (diagrams, screenshots, tables, architecture figures) in the source PDFs must be mapped in the benchmark visual index. Each mapped visual must have a unique identifier, source PDF reference, page number, caption or description, and a list of requirements derived from or informed by that visual.

Source hashes (SHA-256) must be recorded for every source document in the inventory. The hash verification process must be automated and re-runnable. Any mismatch between recorded hash and computed hash must be flagged as a data integrity warning.

Per-PDF semantic coverage must be computed and recorded, measuring what fraction of each source document's substantive content has been reflected in requirements, ontology entries, or test specifications. Documents with less than 80% semantic coverage must be flagged for review. Coverage must be measured by mapping document sections to requirements or ontology artifacts.

Generic patterns (command syntax structures, field types, naming conventions, data formats) extracted from the source documents must be catalogued in the ontology. Pattern extraction must identify recurring structures rather than one-off examples. Each pattern entry must include the pattern itself, example instances, source document references, and confidence level (explicit, inferred, or speculative).

Product-specific boundary enforcement must identify which ontology entries and requirements apply only to specific BenchCore or NRCS product variants versus those that generalize. Any claim of generality must be cross-referenced against at least two independent source documents. Terms and requirements that are explicitly product-scoped must be labeled with their applicable product context.

The ontology must provide comprehensive coverage of all domain terms, acronyms, protocol terms, command terms, UI terms, and ML terms referenced across the source documents. Each term in the ontology must be traceable to at least one source document. The ontology must include at least 25 acronyms, 20 glossary terms, 15 protocol terms, 15 command terms, 10 UI terms, and 15 ML terms.

Full traceability must exist from every requirement to its source documents (via source_ids), to its test cases (positive, negative, and sabotage), and to its verification status. The traceability matrix must be queryable by requirement ID, source document, test name, and verification status. All source_ids must resolve to valid document entries in the inventory.

The evaluation harness must support configuration-driven test execution. Configuration must specify the evaluation mode (single-line, multi-line, or both), the set of target fields to evaluate, the metrics to compute (F1, MRR, Top-K, exact-match), and the evaluation dataset split to use. Configuration must be provided in JSON format and must be validated against a schema.

The evaluation harness must compute macro-averaged F1 score for each target field and report per-field and aggregate results. Confidence intervals must be computed using bootstrap resampling with at least 1000 resamples at the 95% confidence level. Results must be output in JSON and human-readable table format.

The evaluation harness must support MRR and Top-K evaluation for ranking-based prediction tasks. K values of 1, 3, and 5 must be supported. MRR must be computed across all evaluation examples, and per-query MRR must be logged for debugging.

The evaluation harness must support evaluation on multiple dataset splits: train, validation, and test. The split configuration must be specified in the evaluation config. Results must be reported separately for each split. The harness must verify that there is no leakage between training and test splits at the story or rundown level.

The evaluation harness must produce a human-readable evaluation report that includes: benchmark version, evaluation configuration, date and time, per-field metric tables, aggregate scores, bootstrap confidence intervals, and a summary of warnings or anomalies detected during evaluation.

The evaluation harness must support both single-line and multi-line prediction modes. In single-line mode, the classifier receives only the current line as input. In multi-line mode, the classifier receives a configurable window of preceding and following lines. The mode must be selectable in the evaluation configuration, not hardcoded.

The evaluation harness must implement sabotage test execution mode that runs positive, negative, and sabotage test suites separately and reports pass/fail for each. Sabotage tests are designed to detect overclaimed capability by providing adversarial inputs that should cause graceful failure rather than incorrect confident predictions.

Feedback policies must define how explicit signals (accept, reject, modify, rate) and implicit signals (dwell time, selection order, modification patterns) are collected, stored, and interpreted. The feedback policy document must specify signal processing pipelines, storage schema, and privacy considerations. Stage A must define the policy but may defer implementation of the live feedback pipeline.

Explicit signal collection must define the data schema for each signal type: accept (prediction_id, timestamp, user_id, context_snapshot), reject (prediction_id, timestamp, user_id, reason if provided), modify (prediction_id, original_value, corrected_value, timestamp, user_id), and rate (prediction_id, rating_value, timestamp, user_id). All schemas must include versioning for forward compatibility.

Implicit signal interpretation must account for position bias by recording the display order of suggestions along with user selection. The feedback pipeline must normalize implicit signals against position bias using at least one documented debiasing method. The debiasing approach must be specified in the feedback policy.

Feedback data must be stored in a queryable format with at least: prediction_id, original_prediction, user_correction (if any), feedback_type, timestamp, user_id, and context_hash. The feedback store must support retrieval by prediction_id and by time range. Storage must use an append-only log structure to preserve audit trail integrity.

Feedback aggregation policies must define how multiple feedback signals on the same prediction are combined. The policy must specify: weighting of explicit vs implicit signals, recency weighting (newer signals weighted higher), and conflict resolution when signals disagree. The aggregation policy must be documented and configurable.

Privacy requirements for feedback data must be defined, including: user_id anonymization or pseudonymization for aggregate analysis, data retention limits, and opt-out mechanisms. The privacy policy must comply with relevant data protection principles. No feedback data used for evaluation may contain personally identifiable information.

The feedback loop must support exploration by occasionally presenting alternative predictions to gather feedback on non-optimal candidates. The exploration policy must specify: exploration rate (epsilon), exploration strategy (epsilon-greedy, Thompson sampling, or other), and conditions under which exploration is reduced or suspended. Stage A must document the exploration policy but may defer adaptive exploration implementation.

Dynamic retrieval must support fetching relevant context, command templates, and historical examples at inference time. The retrieval system must accept a rundown context (one or more lines) as input and return ranked results from a retrieval index. The index must support at least one of: vector similarity search, keyword search, or hybrid search.

The capability registry must catalog all available prediction capabilities, command types, field definitions, and their metadata. Each registry entry must include: capability_id, name, description, input_schema, output_schema, example_usage, and source_document_refs. The registry must support queries by capability name, description text, and command type.

Natural language to ID mapping must support converting free-text descriptions of desired commands into capability registry IDs. The mapping must handle synonym variation (e.g., 'sports graphic' and 'score overlay' mapping to the same capability). Confidence scores must accompany mapping results so that low-confidence mappings can be flagged for human review.

The retrieval index must be populated from curated rundown examples and command templates. Index population must be a reproducible, scripted process that does not require live data sources. The index must be versioned so that evaluation results can be traced to a specific index version.

Retrieval-augmented prediction must concatenate retrieved examples into the LLM prompt in a structured format. The prompt template must include clear delimiters between the input context and retrieved examples. The number of retrieved examples (top-K) must be configurable. The retrieval system must not exceed the LLM's context window; if results would exceed the limit, fewer examples must be returned.

The learning policy must enforce the Stage A constraint that the LLM remains frozen — no parameter updates, fine-tuning, LoRA adaptation, or any form of weight modification are permitted. All learning must occur through non-parametric means: prompt engineering, example selection, retrieval index updates, and feedback-driven reranking. Violations of the frozen LLM constraint render the configuration ineligible for Stage A evaluation.

Prompt engineering must be used as the primary mechanism for adapting the frozen LLM to BenchCore prediction tasks. Prompts must specify: the task description, the input format (rundown context lines), the output format (structured prediction), the field definitions, and any formatting constraints. Prompt templates must be versioned and stored separately from code.

Example selection must support choosing relevant few-shot examples from the retrieval index based on similarity to the current input context. The selection strategy must be documented and configurable (e.g., k-nearest-neighbors, diversity sampling, or random sampling for exploration). Example selection is the primary non-parametric learning mechanism in Stage A.

Drift detection must monitor prediction quality metrics over time and alert when significant degradation is observed. The drift detection policy must specify: the metrics to monitor, the window size for trend computation, the alerting threshold (e.g., F1 decrease > 0.05 over 7 days), and the response procedure when drift is detected. Stage A must define drift detection policy but may defer automated drift response.

Confidence calibration must be applied to all predictions such that the reported confidence score reflects the expected accuracy. Calibration quality must be measured using expected calibration error (ECE). Predictions with confidence below a configurable threshold must either be withheld or flagged for mandatory human review. The threshold must be documented in the learning policy.

The learning policy must document which ML techniques and model architectures are allowed, forbidden, or deferred for Stage A. Allowed techniques include: prompt engineering, dynamic retrieval, example selection, feature extraction, non-parametric classification, and confidence calibration. Forbidden techniques include: any form of weight training, fine-tuning, LoRA, model distillation, and meta-learning. Deferred techniques include: reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF), online learning, and active learning.

Grammar validation must check that generated commands conform to the defined syntax rules for their command type. Validation must cover: correct prefix usage (*CG, *OD), proper delimiter placement, valid argument types, required vs optional fields, and value range constraints. Validation errors must be reported with specific error messages indicating which rule was violated.

Grammar rules must be extracted from source documents and codified in a machine-readable format (JSON). Rules must cover: command types and their syntax, field definitions (name, type, required/optional, allowed values), delimiter conventions, comment syntax, and continuation line markers. Each grammar rule must be traceable to its source document.

AST-based validation must parse generated commands into an abstract syntax tree and verify structural correctness at the tree level. The AST parser must support all defined command types and produce meaningful error trees for invalid inputs. AST validation must catch structural errors that string-level validation would miss, such as nested command relationships and cross-field dependencies.

Semantic parsing of rundown text into structured command representations must handle: keyword detection (identifying command-relevant terms), entity extraction (pulling field values from free text), and ambiguity resolution (choosing among multiple valid parses). The parser must produce a ranked list of candidate parses when multiple valid interpretations exist.

Keyword expansion must support mapping a set of descriptive keywords to a full command structure via template matching. The keyword-to-template mapping must be extensible and stored separately from the expansion logic. Expansion must include field value inference: inferring the appropriate values for command fields based on the keywords and context, not simply inserting placeholders.

Command syntax documentation must be produced from the grammar rules in human-readable format. The documentation must include: complete syntax specifications for each command type, examples of valid commands, common error patterns, and a reference for all field types and allowed values. Documentation must be generated from the machine-readable grammar rules to ensure consistency.

Grammar validation must be integrated into the prediction pipeline such that every prediction is validated before it is returned or evaluated. Predictions that fail grammar validation must be either corrected automatically if possible or flagged with a validation error. The correction strategy (auto-correct or flag) must be configurable per command type.

Raw rundown log data must be parsed into structured records with the following fields extracted: line_number, raw_text, parsed_command_type, field_values, preceding_line, following_line, story_id, and confidence_in_parse. The parser must handle common log format variations including tab-delimited, space-delimited, and mixed-format lines. Parse failures must be logged but must not crash the pipeline.

Story segmentation must correctly identify story boundaries from rundown data. Segmentation rules must be extracted from source documents and configurable. The segmenter must produce a list of stories with their items, maintaining temporal ordering. Segmentation accuracy must be measurable against a hand-annotated ground-truth set of at least 50 stories.

Field extraction must identify and extract all target fields from parsed rundown items. Field definitions (name, type, allowed values, extraction pattern) must be stored in the ontology. The extractor must handle missing fields, malformed values, and type mismatches gracefully. Extraction quality must be verified against a hand-validated ground-truth dataset.

Data provenance must be recorded for every example in the evaluation dataset, including: source log file, line number range, extraction date, parser version, and any manual corrections applied. Provenance records must be stored in a queryable format and included in evaluation reports. Examples without complete provenance must be excluded from evaluation.

Annotation guidelines must be documented for the ground-truth dataset, specifying: how field values are determined, how ambiguous cases are resolved, inter-annotator agreement targets (minimum 0.8 Cohen's kappa), and the review process for annotation disagreements. The guidelines must be versioned and stored alongside the ground-truth data.

Data versioning must be implemented for all datasets used in the benchmark. Dataset versions must include: a semantic version number, a changelog describing additions and corrections, the SHA-256 hash of the dataset file, and a date. The evaluation harness must record which dataset version was used for each evaluation run and reject runs where the dataset hash does not match the recorded version.

All protocol implementations in the benchmark must be mock implementations. Mock protocol adapters must simulate the behavior of the live protocol (MOS, REST, TCP) without requiring access to external systems. Mock implementations must support documented failure modes including: timeout, malformed payload, connection refused, and authentication failure. No mock may make network calls to external services.

Each mock protocol adapter must have a corresponding protocol contract specification that documents: the interface methods, expected input/output formats, error types, sequencing constraints, and performance characteristics (latency range, throughput limits). The contract must be sufficient for someone to implement a real adapter without additional documentation.

The adapter boundary between the benchmark core and protocol implementations must be defined by a clear interface that allows swapping mock and live implementations without changing internal logic. The boundary interface must define: request serialization, response deserialization, error handling, and connection lifecycle. The benchmark must verify that all internal code uses only the boundary interface, never direct protocol calls.

Human review schemas must define the data structures for presenting predictions to users and capturing their feedback. Schemas must cover: suggestion card content (prediction, alternatives, confidence, context), feedback actions (accept, reject, modify, rate), and review session metadata (user_id, session_id, review_timestamp, prediction_ids). Schemas must be documented in JSON Schema format.

The benchmark must have no live dependencies on external systems. All data, services, and infrastructure required to run the benchmark must be self-contained within the benchmark directory. The benchmark must be fully runnable offline after initial setup. Any code path that attempts to connect to an external service without a mock must be identified as a violation and fail its tests.

Sabotage tests must be defined for every requirement to detect overclaimed capability. Sabotage tests provide adversarial or edge-case inputs designed to trigger incorrect behavior in overconfident systems. Each requirement with accepted_for_agentx=true must have at least one associated sabotage test. Sabotage tests must be run as part of the evaluation harness and their results reported separately from positive/negative tests.

The benchmark must support clean replay: running the same evaluation configuration multiple times must produce identical results (within floating-point tolerance). Stochastic components (sampling, retrieval) must use seeded randomness with the seed recorded in the evaluation configuration. Any non-determinism in evaluation results must be documented and its magnitude bounded.

The claim boundary must be explicitly documented, specifying: what the benchmark does and does not claim about Agent_X's capabilities, which BenchCore-specific terms are used descriptively (not as Agent_X core features), and the scope limitations (benchmark-only, no production deployment, no live integration). The claim boundary document must be referenced in all evaluation reports and any published results.

The benchmark must document the inverse science methodology used to derive ontology entries, grammar rules, and requirements from raw source documents. The methodology must describe: how raw observations (source text excerpts) are collected, how patterns are identified and generalized, how hypotheses about structure are formed and tested, how abductive reasoning produces candidate grammar rules, how deductive reasoning verifies those rules against examples, and how negative knowledge (known limitations and counterexamples) is captured.

Negative knowledge must be catalogued for the prediction domain, documenting: known limitations of the frozen LLM approach (context window constraints, recency bias, hallucination risks), field types that the classifier cannot reliably predict with Stage A constraints, boundary cases where grammar rules are ambiguous or contradictory, and confidence thresholds below which predictions should not be trusted. The negative knowledge catalogue must be stored as a machine-readable artifact.

Multi-line prediction evaluation must test at least three context window configurations: preceding-1 (one line before the target), symmetric-1 (one line before and after), and full-story (all lines in the current story). Results for each configuration must be reported separately to enable comparison of context window effectiveness. The evaluation must also report oracle-context results where the optimal context window is selected per example.

The Inception plugin architecture must be documented with interface specifications for: prediction request/response, feedback submission, configuration management, and health/status reporting. The plugin interface must define versioned contracts to support backward compatibility. Stage A must document the plugin architecture but defer its implementation; the documentation must clearly state that plugin integration is out of benchmark scope.
